voiceover: You were called too sensitive, too scattered, too much — your whole life. But what if that wasn't your personality? Seven signs of ADHD in women that get dismissed constantly. Emotional meltdowns over small things — that's rejection sensitive dysphoria, not drama. Forgetting conversations you were in. Zoning out mid-sentence. Being brilliant at work but unable to do laundry. Feeling lazy when you're actually exhausted from masking all day. Losing things obsessively. And always feeling like you're failing at being a normal person. You weren't failing. You were undiagnosed. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: 7 Signs of ADHD in Women That Get Dismissed #Shorts
description: ADHD in women looks very different from the textbook definition — and most of it gets written off as personality. From rejection sensitive dysphoria to masking exhaustion, these are seven signs that are common in ADHD and chronically overlooked. If you went undiagnosed for years, this might explain a lot. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Your brain won't start a hard task? Stop forcing it. Bribe it. A dopamine menu is a short list of small rewards you pair with that task before you begin. Step one: write down five to ten things that genuinely light your brain up. A favorite snack, a playlist, a cozy blanket, a candle. Step two: pick one and set it up first, before you open the work. Step three: start the task while that reward is active. Your brain needs dopamine to launch, not discipline. You are not broken. You just need the right fuel. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Use a Dopamine Menu to Start Hard Tasks #Shorts
description: If your ADHD brain refuses to start tasks, the problem is not laziness, it is low dopamine. A dopamine menu is a simple ADHD-friendly technique that pairs small rewards with hard tasks to help your brain launch. Try it today and feel the difference.

voiceover: You thought you knew your ADHD symptoms. You didn't. Forgetting what you walked into a room for — every single time — that's ADHD. Feeling every fabric, every tag, every sound louder than everyone else — that's ADHD. Starting five things and finishing zero before noon — that's ADHD. Lying awake at 2am while your brain holds a TED talk — that's ADHD. And that crushing guilt after one small criticism — rejection sensitive dysphoria — also ADHD. None of this is a personality flaw. It's your nervous system, wired differently. Sound familiar every single day? Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: 5 ADHD Symptoms You Didn't Know Were ADHD #Shorts
description: Think you know your ADHD symptoms? These 5 surprising signs -- from sensory overwhelm to rejection sensitivity to a racing mind at 2am -- are all common in ADHD and rarely talked about. If any of these hit home, your brain is just wired differently -- and that is worth understanding. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Object Permanence — if you can't see it, your brain acts like it doesn't exist. It's not forgetfulness. Your ADHD brain genuinely loses track of things that leave your field of view. Here's what it looks like: You ignore a text for days — it just vanished from your brain. You forget laundry in the machine until it smells. You lose your keys even when they're sitting right there on the counter. Out of sight really does mean out of mind. If this sounds familiar, you're not lazy — your brain just works differently. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: ADHD Object Permanence: Out of Sight, Out of Mind #Shorts
description:
Object Permanence is a common ADHD pattern where your brain genuinely loses track of things it can't see — and it explains so much. From forgotten texts to missing keys in plain sight, this is your brain, not your character. Common in ADHD and finally worth understanding.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

question: Which inbox makes you sweat most?

options.label: The avalanche
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A cozy illustrated email inbox icon overflowing with hundreds of stacked envelope shapes cascading out in a gentle avalanche, piling softly onto a pastel desk surface, envelopes in cream and lavender with small blush pink accents.
options.result: Avoidance is your coping style - unseen stress still charges interest in your brain.

options.label: The one unread
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A single small envelope sitting alone on a wide empty pastel shelf, with a tiny bold dot badge above it glowing softly in blush pink, surrounded by lots of empty calm cream-colored space that somehow feels more tense than crowded.
options.result: One unread haunts you more than a thousand - your brain treats it like an alarm that won't quit.

options.label: All flagged, none done
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A cozy illustrated pile of envelopes each tagged with a small soft sage green flag or ribbon bookmark, every single envelope flagged and stacked neatly but clearly untouched, the flags fluttering gently, a system that looks organized but has stalled completely.
options.result: You have brilliant systems that work perfectly right up until they don't - classic ADHD intention loop.

options.label: Already archived
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A clean empty inbox shelf with a single small open archive box beside it, all envelopes tucked neatly inside the box, the shelf surface bare and glowing in soft cream light, a little dusty label on the box, the whole scene radiating a slightly-too-tidy energy.
options.result: Out of sight, out of mind is your survival strategy - and yes, some of those still needed replies.

introVoiceover: Four inboxes. Pick the one that makes your stomach drop fastest. Your choice actually says something real about how your ADHD brain handles stress - drop your answer in the comments.
outroVoiceover: No wrong answers - just different ADHD brains doing their best. Follow for daily ADHD content.
title: Which inbox stresses you out most? (ADHD test) #Shorts
description:
Pick the inbox that makes you sweat - then watch as we reveal what each choice says about your ADHD brain, one at a time. The results might feel uncomfortably accurate.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Someone gives you mild feedback and your whole body floods with shame. That's not being sensitive. That's rejection sensitive dysphoria. RSD is one of the most intense emotional experiences tied to ADHD, and almost nobody talks about it. Here's the science: ADHD brains have dysregulated dopamine and norepinephrine, the chemicals that manage emotional signaling. So when rejection, or even the hint of it, hits your brain, it doesn't land like a nudge. It lands like a shockwave. The pain is real, it's neurological, and it is not a character flaw. RSD can make you avoid asking for help, quit things before anyone can judge you, or replay one comment for three days straight. So what actually helps? First, name it in the moment. Saying 'this is RSD, not reality' interrupts the spiral. Second, delay your response by ten minutes. The flood passes faster than it feels like it will. Third, build one safe relationship where honesty feels okay. Your nervous system learns safety slowly, but it does learn. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Why Criticism Physically Hurts With ADHD #Shorts
description:
Rejection sensitive dysphoria is one of the most painful and least talked about parts of ADHD. Learn why criticism hits so hard neurologically and three concrete steps to interrupt the spiral. Common in ADHD, and completely real.
Follow for daily ADHD content.
